summary: Matched Yoshi guard function ftYs_Shield_8012C914 and promoted src/melee/ft/chara/ftYoshi/ftYs_Guard.c from NonMatching to Matching. The PR also corrected Yoshi guard motion-var layout/naming enough to reuse the existing guard union member, replaced externed float constants with literal 0.0F/1.0F where needed, and moved a block of Yoshi SpecialS data out of ftYs_Guard.c into ftYs_SpecialS.c with corresponding split-range updates. decomp.dev reported one new match, +440 matched bytes, and +3788 linked bytes.
what_changed: Implemented ftYs_Shield_8012C914, previously left as a nonmatching stub marker, including a motion-state change to ftYs_MS_GuardOn_1, ftAnim_8006EBA4, input timer/flag setup, guard common-data timers, ftCo_8009370C callback setup, and an inlined initialization helper.

Added static inline ftYs_Shield_8012C914_inline to perform the repeated guard initialization sequence: disable hurt capsules, clear/reset Yoshi guard motion variables, reset a joint translation to zero, call ftYs_Init_8012B8A4, ftCo_80091D58, and ft_PlaySFX.
Changed configure.py so melee/ft/chara/ftYoshi/ftYs_Guard.c is built as Object(Matching) instead of Object(NonMatching).
Adjusted config/GALE01/splits.txt so ftYs_Guard.c data ends at 0x803CED48 and ftYs_SpecialS.c owns the data range 0x803CED48-0x803CEDBC.
Moved static data ftYs_Unk3_803CED84, ftYs_Unk3_803CED94, and ftYs_Unk3_803CEDA4 from ftYs_Guard.c to ftYs_SpecialS.c, matching the updated data split ownership.
Removed the generic ftYoshi_MotionVars.unk2 struct and updated users in ftYs_Guard.c to access ftYoshi_MotionVars.guard instead.
Refined ftYoshi_GuardVars layout in types.h: xC is a bool at fp+234C, x10/x14/x18 are f32, x20/x24 are ints, and padding was adjusted.
Replaced extern references to ftYs_Init_804D9A28 and ftYs_Init_804D9A2C with direct 1.0F and 0.0F literals in guard animation and motion-state code.
smart_moves: The match was achieved by recognizing that the unknown motion-vars struct was actually Yoshi guard state and consolidating users onto mv.ys.guard, reducing duplicate/ambiguous union members.
Using a static inline helper for ftYs_Shield_8012C914_inline preserved the compiler shape of a repeated initialization sequence while keeping the new matched function readable.
The PR linked the whole ftYs_Guard object only after aligning both code and data boundaries, avoiding a partial object-level mismatch caused by SpecialS-owned data sitting in Guard.
Replacing externed 0.0/1.0 float constants with literals simplified dependencies and better matched the surrounding generated code in this file.
The split-file update and data relocation were kept together with the matching change, which is important because object matching can depend on exact section ownership, not just function text.
decomp_lessons: When a fighter file fails to link after a function match, inspect adjacent .data ownership; static data may belong to the next split/source file even if it was temporarily parked nearby.
Motion-var union members should be named for the action state once enough uses are known. Removing an unknown alias can expose the real layout and prevent field-offset drift.
Do not preserve extern float constants solely because an earlier nonmatching version used them; small literal constants may be required for the correct instruction sequence.
Repeated setup logic can sometimes need an inline helper rather than a normal helper call or copy-pasted block to match the original compiler's inlining decisions.
Field types matter for matching: changing UNK_T/s32 placeholders to bool, f32, and int at known offsets can affect generated loads/stores and make later guard functions easier to match.
naming_conventions: Yoshi guard motion variables are accessed as fp->mv.ys.guard rather than fp->mv.ys.unk2 once identified.
The new guard helper was named ftYs_Shield_8012C914_inline, following the project convention of anchoring still-unnamed helpers to the known function address/name plus an inline suffix.
Matched Yoshi guard callbacks retain existing ftYs_GuardOn_*, ftYs_GuardHold_*, ftYs_GuardOff_*, ftYs_GuardDamage_*, and ftYs_Shield_* naming patterns.
Unknown static data names remained address-based, e.g. ftYs_Unk3_803CED84, because the PR evidence does not identify semantic names for those Vec4/Vec3 constants.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Promoted ftYs_Guard.c to Matching only after ftYs_Shield_8012C914 reached 100% and the object's data split was corrected.
Used direct 1.0F increments for guard frame counters and 0.0F/1.0F motion-state parameters to match the emitted code.
Introduced an inline initialization helper to reproduce original inlining around ftYs_Shield_8012C914.
Adjusted struct padding and field types around fp+2340 through fp+2364 so guard variable accesses use the expected offsets.
Moved three static vector constants to ftYs_SpecialS.c and updated splits so .data section boundaries match the linked object layout.
review_feedback: No human review comments were present in the provided slice.
decomp.dev bot reported: matched code 46.95% (+0.01%, +440 bytes), linked code 28.43% (+0.10%, +3788 bytes), and one new match for main/melee/ft/chara/ftYoshi/ftYs_Guard::ftYs_Shield_8012C914.
follow_up_queries: Look for later PRs that rename ftYs_Shield_8012C914 or ftYs_Shield_8012C914_inline with semantic guard-action names.
Search for subsequent refinements to ftYoshi_GuardVars fields x0, xC, x10, x14, x18, x20, and x24.
Check whether ftYs_SpecialS.c later matches functions using ftYs_Unk3_803CED84, ftYs_Unk3_803CED94, or ftYs_Unk3_803CEDA4.
Compare other fighter guard implementations for the same ftCo_8009370C and hurt-capsule reset pattern.
